subject: math
title: Word Problems with Ratios
content:
Word Problems with Ratios

What is a Ratio?

A ratio compares two quantities. It tells us how much of one thing there is compared to another.

Ways to Write Ratios

• Using "to" — 3 to 4
• Using a colon — 3:4
• As a fraction — 3/4

Ratios in Real Life

• Recipe: 2 cups flour to 1 cup sugar (2:1)
• Map scale: 1 inch = 100 miles
• Class ratio: 12 girls to 10 boys (12:10)

Simplifying Ratios

Just like fractions, ratios can be simplified. Divide both numbers by their greatest common factor (GCF).

Example: Simplify 12:8
• Both divisible by 4
• 12 ÷ 4 = 3, 8 ÷ 4 = 2
• Simplified ratio: 3:2

Equivalent Ratios

Multiply or divide both parts by the same number to get equivalent ratios.

• 2:3 = 4:6 = 6:9 = 10:15

Solving Ratio Problems

Use "what I do to one side, I do to the other!"

Word Problem:
"The ratio of blue marbles to red marbles is 3:5. If there are 15 red marbles, how many blue marbles are there?"

Solution:• Ratio is 3:5
• To go from 5 to 15, multiply by 3
• Do the same to 3 × 3 = 9 blue marbles

Word Problem:
"A recipe makes 12 cookies using 2 cups of flour. How many cups of flour are needed for 30 cookies?"

Solution:
• 12 to 30 is multiplied by 2.5 (not a whole number, so use fractions)
• Actually: 12 cookies = 2 cups, so 1 cookie = 2/12 = 1/6 cup
• 30 cookies = 30 × 1/6 = 5 cups of flour

questions.hint: Divide both numbers by their GCF (4)

questions.prompt: The ratio of apples to oranges in a basket is 3:4. If there are 9 apples, how many oranges are there?
If the ratio of boys to girls is 2:3 and there are 20 boys, how many girls?
A map has a scale of 1:100. If two cities are 3.5 inches apart on the map, how far apart are they in real life?
